title: Heuristics, greedy search and A*

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Heuristics, greedy search and A*; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Fundamental Issues and Basic Search Strategies: state spaces, uninformed/informed, and adversarial search; atomic topic: Heuristics, greedy search and A*
sources.claim: A heuristic estimates remaining cost; greedy best-first prioritizes that estimate alone, while A* prioritizes accumulated cost plus estimate and inherits optimality only under stated admissibility or consistency conditions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A heuristic estimates remaining cost; greedy best-first prioritizes that estimate alone, while A* prioritizes accumulated cost plus estimate and inherits optimality only under stated admissibility or consistency conditions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Manhattan distance is used on a four-direction unit grid without teleportation and never exceeds the true remaining path cost. Your goal is to implement A* and compare path cost and nodes expanded under two heuristics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Agent, goal and utility models; dsa graphs bfs dfs; Uninformed search; dsa graphs shortest path. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A heuristic estimates remaining cost; greedy best-first prioritizes that estimate alone, while A* prioritizes accumulated cost plus estimate and inherits optimality only under stated admissibility or consistency conditions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: An admissible heuristic can still expand many states, inconsistency can require reopening, tie-breaking changes work not guaranteed cost, scaling must match costs, and an empirical estimate is not automatically admissible. The learner must reject this overclaim: A heuristic that often predicts accurately is admissible, and A* always returns an optimal path regardless of heuristic or duplicate handling.

includes: Design heuristic estimates and relate admissibility or consistency to optimality guarantees.
Implement A* and compare path cost and nodes expanded under two heuristics.
excludes: An admissible heuristic can still expand many states, inconsistency can require reopening, tie-breaking changes work not guaranteed cost, scaling must match costs, and an empirical estimate is not automatically admissible.

outcomes: Implement A* and compare path cost and nodes expanded under two heuristics.

examples.title: Heuristics, greedy search and A*: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Adding a multiplier greater than one expands fewer nodes on examples but removes the standard admissibility guarantee, so the trade-off is reported honestly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Adding a multiplier greater than one expands fewer nodes on examples but removes the standard admissibility guarantee, so the trade-off is reported honestly.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: An admissible heuristic can still expand many states, inconsistency can require reopening, tie-breaking changes work not guaranteed cost, scaling must match costs, and an empirical estimate is not automatically admissible.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A heuristic that often predicts accurately is admissible, and A* always returns an optimal path regardless of heuristic or duplicate handling.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A heuristic that often predicts accurately is admissible, and A* always returns an optimal path regardless of heuristic or duplicate handling.

analogy.example: a route planner that tracks states, legal moves, accumulated cost, estimates, and an ordered frontier can introduce Heuristics, greedy search and A* only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: state-space representation, frontier policies, heuristics, and adversarial backup
limitations: real environments can be continuous, stochastic, changing, partially observed, or too large for the simplified route model
